title: Linux Foundations Assessment
description: Comprehensive assessment covering all Linux fundamentals. Pass this to earn your Linux Foundations certificate.

questions.question: What percentage of the world's top servers run Linux?
questions.explanation: Linux powers over 96.3% of the world's top 1 million servers.

questions.question: What is the relationship between the Linux kernel and a Linux distribution?
options: The kernel is the core; a distro bundles it with tools and a package manager
A distro is a lighter version of the kernel
The kernel is only for advanced users
questions.explanation: The Linux kernel manages hardware and processes. A distribution packages the kernel with user-space tools, libraries, and a package manager.

questions.question: The 'pwd' command displays the current working directory.
questions.explanation: 'pwd' stands for 'print working directory' and shows the full path of your current location.

questions.question: Which command lists directory contents including hidden files?
questions.explanation: The '-a' (all) flag shows hidden files that start with a dot.

questions.question: What does the permission string 'rwxr-x---' mean?
options: Owner can read/write/execute, group can read/execute, others have no access
Everyone can read, write, and execute
Only the owner can access the file
questions.explanation: Permissions are split into 3 groups of 3: owner (rwx), group (r-x), others (---). Owner has full access, group can read and execute, others have no permissions.

questions.question: Which command changes file permissions in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'chmod' (change mode) modifies file permissions. 'chown' changes ownership, 'chgrp' changes group.

questions.question: In Linux, the root directory is represented by '~'.
questions.explanation: The root directory is '/'. The tilde '~' represents the current user's home directory.

questions.question: What does the pipe operator '|' do in Linux?
options: Sends the output of one command as input to another
questions.explanation: The pipe operator takes stdout of the left command and passes it as stdin to the right command, enabling command composition.

questions.question: Which command searches for text patterns in files?
questions.explanation: 'grep' (Global Regular Expression Print) searches for text patterns within files. 'find' searches for files by name/attributes.

questions.question: What is the purpose of the /etc directory in Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc contains system-wide configuration files. It's one of the most important directories for system administration.

questions.question: Docker containers share the host Linux kernel.
questions.explanation: Docker containers share the host OS kernel, which is why they're lightweight compared to virtual machines.

questions.question: Which package manager does Ubuntu use?
questions.explanation: Ubuntu uses apt (Advanced Package Tool). yum/dnf are for Red Hat/Fedora, pacman is for Arch Linux.

questions.question: What does 'chmod 755 script.sh' do?
options: Makes the file read-only for everyone
Owner: rwx, Group: r-x, Others: r-x
Makes the file writable by everyone
questions.explanation: 755 in octal means: 7 (rwx) for owner, 5 (r-x) for group, 5 (r-x) for others. This is a common permission for executable scripts.

questions.question: Which command shows running processes in Linux?
questions.explanation: 'top' shows real-time information about running processes, CPU usage, and memory. 'htop' is a popular enhanced alternative.

questions.question: What is the significance of the shebang (#!) at the beginning of a script?
options: It specifies which interpreter should execute the script
It marks the file as executable
It's required syntax in all scripts
questions.explanation: The shebang line (e.g., #!/bin/bash) tells the OS which interpreter to use when executing the script directly.

questions.question: The 'man' command displays the manual page for a given command.
questions.explanation: 'man' (manual) provides detailed documentation for Linux commands, including options, syntax, and examples.

questions.question: What does the command 'tail -f /var/log/syslog' do?
options: Shows the first 10 lines of the file
Follows the file in real-time, showing new lines as they're added
Counts the number of lines in the file
questions.explanation: 'tail -f' (follow) continuously monitors a file and displays new lines as they are appended. Essential for real-time log monitoring.

questions.question: Which Linux philosophy principle states that programs should do one thing well?
questions.explanation: The Unix/Linux philosophy emphasizes writing programs that do one thing and do it well, then combining them with pipes and redirection.

questions.question: What does the 'sudo' command do?
options: Executes a command with superuser (root) privileges
questions.explanation: 'sudo' (superuser do) executes a command with elevated privileges. It's safer than logging in as root because it provides an audit trail.

questions.question: Which redirection operator appends output to a file without overwriting?
questions.explanation: '>>' appends output to the end of a file. '>' overwrites the file. '<' redirects input from a file. '|' pipes output to another command.

questions.question: What is the first process that starts during Linux boot (PID 1)?
questions.explanation: systemd (or init on older systems) is the first user-space process started by the kernel. It has PID 1 and is the parent of all other processes.

questions.question: What is the correct command to add a user to a supplementary group without removing them from existing groups?
questions.explanation: The -a (append) flag with -G is critical. Without -a, usermod -G replaces ALL supplementary groups. Always use -aG together.

questions.question: A symbolic link (symlink) still works after the original target file is deleted.
questions.explanation: When the target of a symlink is deleted, the symlink becomes a 'broken' or 'dangling' link. Hard links, on the other hand, continue to work because they share the same inode.

questions.question: What does the SUID bit (set on an executable file) do?
options: Runs the program with the permissions of the file owner
Prevents the file from being deleted
questions.explanation: SUID (Set User ID) causes the program to run with the permissions of the file's owner, not the user who executes it. The classic example is /usr/bin/passwd which runs as root.

questions.question: What is the numeric permission value for chmod that gives owner rwx, group rx, and others no access?
questions.explanation: r=4, w=2, x=1. Owner: 4+2+1=7, Group: 4+0+1=5, Others: 0+0+0=0. So 750 means rwxr-x---.

questions.question: Which file stores password hashes on Linux?
questions.explanation: /etc/shadow stores encrypted password hashes and is readable only by root. /etc/passwd stores user account information but no longer contains actual passwords.

questions.question: Which systemctl command starts a service AND enables it to start on boot?
questions.explanation: The --now flag combined with enable both enables the service for future boots and starts it immediately. This is equivalent to running 'enable' and 'start' separately.

questions.question: The 'kill -9' signal can be caught and handled by the target process.
questions.explanation: SIGKILL (signal 9) cannot be caught, blocked, or handled by a process. It's an immediate, forceful termination by the kernel. Always try SIGTERM (15) first.

questions.question: In the 'free -h' command output, which column best represents memory available for new processes?
questions.explanation: The 'available' column shows memory that can be used by new processes, including reclaimable cache. The 'free' column only shows completely unused RAM, which is misleading because Linux uses free RAM for caching.

questions.question: Which command shows systemd journal logs for a specific service?
questions.explanation: journalctl -u filters journal entries by systemd unit (service). Add -f to follow in real-time, --since for time filtering, and -p for priority filtering.

questions.question: What does the sticky bit on a directory prevent?
options: Anyone from reading files in the directory
Users from deleting other users' files
questions.explanation: The sticky bit on a directory (like /tmp) means only the file owner or root can delete or rename files, even if others have write permission to the directory.

questions.question: What does LVM stand for and what is its main advantage?
options: Linux Version Manager — manages kernel versions
Logical Volume Manager — allows flexible disk volume resizing
Local Virtual Machine — runs lightweight VMs
Log Volume Monitor — monitors disk logs
questions.explanation: Logical Volume Manager adds a flexible layer between physical disks and filesystems. Its main advantage is live resizing of volumes without downtime.

questions.question: In /etc/fstab, it is recommended to use UUID instead of device paths like /dev/sdb1.
questions.explanation: Device names (like /dev/sdb) can change between reboots if disks are added or reordered. UUIDs are unique and persistent identifiers that always point to the same filesystem.

questions.question: Which command shows listening TCP ports along with the process name?
questions.explanation: ss -tlnp shows TCP (-t) listening (-l) ports in numeric format (-n) with process names (-p). This is the go-to command for checking which services are running on which ports.

questions.question: What is the recommended order for sending signals to stop a process?
options: SIGTERM first, wait, then SIGKILL if needed
questions.explanation: Always send SIGTERM (15) first to allow graceful shutdown. Only use SIGKILL (9) as a last resort, because it prevents the process from cleaning up resources.

questions.question: Which section of a systemd unit file defines the 'Restart=on-failure' directive?
questions.explanation: The [Service] section defines how the service runs, including ExecStart, Restart policy, User, WorkingDirectory, and environment variables.

questions.question: What does the command 'find / -perm -4000 -type f' search for?
options: Files modified in the last 4000 minutes
questions.explanation: The permission mask 4000 corresponds to the SUID bit. This command finds all regular files with SUID set, which is important for security auditing.

questions.question: The umask value is subtracted from the default permissions to determine the actual permissions of newly created files.
questions.explanation: Files default to 666 and directories to 777. With umask 022, new files get 644 (666-022) and new directories get 755 (777-022).

questions.question: In awk, what does '$NF' refer to?
options: The number of fields in the current line
The last field in the current line
questions.explanation: NF is the number of fields. $NF accesses the field at that position, which is the last field. For example, if a line has 5 fields, $NF equals $5.

questions.question: What does the cron schedule '0 2 * * 0' mean?
questions.explanation: Cron format: minute(0) hour(2) day(*) month(*) day-of-week(0=Sunday). So this runs at 2:00 AM every Sunday.

questions.question: Which sed command removes all empty lines from a file?
options: Both the first and third options
questions.explanation: sed '/^$/d' deletes lines matching the empty pattern (^$). sed -n '/./p' prints only lines containing at least one character. Both effectively remove empty lines.

questions.question: What is the primary purpose of SSH key-based authentication over password authentication?
options: It's immune to brute-force attacks and doesn't transmit secrets
It allows multiple users to share one account
questions.explanation: SSH keys use asymmetric cryptography. The private key never leaves your machine, making it immune to brute-force attacks and network sniffing. Keys are also much stronger than passwords.

questions.question: The 'df -i' command checks inode usage, which can be exhausted even when disk space is available.
questions.explanation: Each file requires an inode. A filesystem can run out of inodes (e.g., millions of tiny files) while still having free disk space. df -i checks inode usage.

questions.question: When load average exceeds the number of CPU cores, what does it indicate?
options: Processes are waiting in queue for CPU time
questions.explanation: Load average represents the number of processes wanting CPU time. When it exceeds the core count, processes must wait in queue, indicating CPU contention.

questions.question: Which tool should you always use to edit /etc/sudoers?
questions.explanation: visudo validates the syntax before saving. A syntax error in /etc/sudoers can lock you out of sudo entirely. Never edit it directly with a regular text editor.
